summary: I traveled from San Francisco to Los Angeles once, and during that trip, some children called me 'Jap,' which upset me. Other than that, I didn't have many experiences there. It was just part of my journey in America.

I was married in the San Francisco Reformed Church, and I also owned an art goods shop in Chinatown there before the Great Depression forced me to close it. San Francisco was a significant place in my life, both for family and business. It was also near where we were taken to the Tanforan Assembly Center during the internment.

My wife and I were originally from Japan, where we belonged to the same church and where she was the minister's daughter. Our children stayed in Japan for three years to visit my wife's gravely ill mother. Although I lived in America, Japan remained important to our family ties and faith.

During World War I, I lived on a farm in the country and did not serve, but I bought war bonds and prayed for peace. I was aware of the conflict but was more focused on my family and farm life at that time. It was a distant event in my life compared to later struggles.

The Great Depression hit me hard when I had to close my art goods shop in San Francisco's Chinatown. It was a difficult time financially, but I managed to keep my family together and focused on their education. Despite the hardships, I stayed determined to provide for my children.

I was prepared for the relocation of Japanese Americans and was not surprised when we were sent to the camps. We first went to Tanforan Assembly Center and then to Topaz War Relocation Center in Utah. Although conditions were harsh and food poor, I found comfort in religious freedom and community support during that difficult time.

We were taken to Tanforan Assembly Center, a former race track near San Francisco, where conditions were incomplete and chaotic. The food was poor and there was no civil government, which made the experience very difficult. It was only a temporary stop before we were moved to Topaz in Utah.

At Topaz in Utah, life was tense; our pastor, Rev. Taro Goto, was even assaulted because some thought he was a spy. Professor Chiura Obata was also attacked, showing how high emotions ran. Despite this, we had religious freedom, schools opened for the children, and I found time for reflection and prayer during our stay.

After leaving Topaz, I moved to Utah, first to Salt Lake City and then to nearby Sandy and Bountiful. Utah became our new home where I farmed and worked in floriculture. The people were friendly, and there was little discrimination, which helped us rebuild our lives after the war.

In Sandy, Utah, I rented five acres of land to farm and even tried mink farming alongside growing vegetables. It was a new start for me and my wife after the war. Sandy provided the space and opportunity to make a living and continue our farming project.

I went to Salt Lake City after leaving Topaz to look for work and eventually transferred my church membership there. The city is the headquarters of Mormonism, but I found the people generally friendly and free of discrimination. Salt Lake City became an important place for my family’s new beginning.

Two years after moving to Utah, we relocated to Bountiful on the recommendation of Rev. T. Ota. There, I worked as a grower for XK Lund Florist and also grew flowers on my own. I worked full time until I was 65 and then part time, enjoying the work and the community support.

My wife and I belonged to the Church of Christ in Japan, and after moving to Utah, we transferred our membership to the Salt Lake Church of Christ. The church welcomed us warmly and helped us in many ways during our resettlement. It was a source of spiritual comfort and community throughout my life in America.

We were married in the San Francisco Reformed Church but later attended the S. F. Methodist Church. During our time at Topaz, our pastor, Rev. Taro Goto of the S. F. Methodist Church, was assaulted, which was a difficult time for our congregation. Despite challenges, the Methodist Church provided religious services and comfort in the camps.

I was on the office staff of the Japanese American newspaper and knew about the Japanese American Citizens League's activities. Although I did not personally receive help from any organizations, I was aware of their role during the time of relocation. I prepared myself for the internment and followed government orders without resistance.

Rev. Taro Goto was our pastor at the S. F. Methodist Church and later at Topaz. He was assaulted one night at Topaz because some people suspected him of being a spy, which was a troubling incident. Despite this, he continued to provide spiritual leadership to our community during internment.

Professor Chiura Obata was also at Topaz and was attacked during a time of high public tension. Like Rev. Goto, he suffered because of suspicion and fear among the internees. His experience reflected the difficult atmosphere we faced in the camp.

The Nisei, or American-born Japanese, were naturally expected to be loyal to America. My eldest son, Paul, was among the first to volunteer for the United States Army with my consent. Their loyalty during such a difficult time was a source of pride for me and our family.

My eldest son, Paul, volunteered for the United States Army early on, and I supported his decision. It was important for us to show our loyalty to America despite the hardships we faced. His service was a hopeful sign during the internment period.

Salt Lake City is the headquarters of Mormonism, and although we were not Mormons, I found the people there generally friendly and free from discrimination. This welcoming environment helped us feel more at home after the war. It was a contrast to some of the difficulties we had faced before.
